publisherName: City of Columbus Division of Water
claimSummary: Columbus says customers can enter an address to see estimated public and private service line material and whether the property is in an active or upcoming project area.
reviewerNote: Official Columbus lead service line program page reviewed manually for public-private map detail and project-area lookup wording.

publisherName: City of Columbus Division of Water
claimSummary: Columbus says annual water line material notices are not water quality notices and that a lead or galvanized notice means some portion of the city-owned side, customer-owned side, or both contains lead or galvanized material.
reviewerNote: Official Columbus lead service line notice guidance reviewed manually for annual material notice framing.

publisherName: City of Columbus Division of Water
claimSummary: Columbus says it started street-by-street replacement in 2025, will replace lead and galvanized water service lines at no direct cost when construction reaches an eligible street, and requires a signed work agreement for participation.
reviewerNote: Official Columbus scheduled replacement program page reviewed manually for 2025 launch timing and work-agreement rules.

publisherName: City of Columbus Division of Water
claimSummary: Columbus says LEAP is a no-interest deferred repayment program with no upfront cost that property owners can use to replace a lead or galvanized service line proactively or when it is leaking.
reviewerNote: Official Columbus LEAP program page reviewed manually for no-upfront-cost proactive and leaking-line replacement language.

publisherName: City of Columbus Division of Water
claimSummary: Columbus says replacement may cost $6000 to $10000 per property when the city cannot replace the line for free under the scheduled program and that LEAP is available as a no-upfront-cost deferred repayment path outside scheduled replacement.
reviewerNote: Official Columbus cost language reviewed manually.

claimSummary: Minneapolis says its lead service line map is updated daily, customers can search an address, and the map uses lead non-lead and unknown symbols.
reviewerNote: Official Minneapolis lead service line map page reviewed manually for daily map updates and symbol guidance.

claimSummary: Minneapolis tells customers that unknown means there is a valid address but the matching water service line record is not available and encourages further research requests when the map shows unknown.
reviewerNote: Official Minneapolis lead service line map guidance reviewed manually for unknown-record meaning and further research workflow.

claimSummary: Minneapolis says the city is replacing lead water service lines across the city, that state and federal funds are paying for the work at no cost to property owners, and that the project follows the 2033 state goal and 2037 federal requirement.
reviewerNote: Official Minneapolis replacement project page reviewed manually for no-cost funding and 2033/2037 timing.

claimSummary: Minneapolis says state and federal funds are paying for lead water service line replacement at no cost to property owners in eligible project areas.
reviewerNote: Official Minneapolis no-cost replacement language reviewed manually for eligible project-area coverage.
